mnemonics.title: Knapsack DP Formula
mnemonics.content: K(i,w) = 'Max of (K(i-1,w), v_i + K(i-1, w-w_i))' - include if fits, else skip!

mnemonics.title: Reverse Order for Space
mnemonics.content: When optimizing 1D Knapsack: 'Going Backward Keeps Items Single' - traverse capacity from W to 0.

mnemonics.title: Subset Sum Base
mnemonics.content: Empty Set = True for sum 0: 'Nothing sums to Nothing (True)'

acronyms.usage: Algorithm paradigm for optimization problems with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Each item can be taken (1) or not taken (0) exactly once
Similar DP structure to Knapsack - another classic optimization problem

visualTips.visualization: Draw table with items as rows (0 to n) and capacity as columns (0 to W). Fill row by row using recurrence. Each cell shows max value achievable.
Similar 2D structure - mark TRUE where subset sum is achievable. First row and column are base cases. Look for TRUE at bottom-right corner.
Imagine 1D array as a single row. Going backward means you only read from 'already computed' cells (from previous item). Forward would overwrite and reuse items.

analogies.analogy: Like packing a suitcase for a trip - each item can only be taken once, you can't put half a shirt in your bag. Want maximum total value within weight limit.
Like checking if you can pay exact fare with coins - each coin can only be used once, can you make exactly $9 using coins {3,4,2}?
Like a student solving textbook problems - instead of solving same problem again, they look up their previous work in a notebook.

analogies.analization: Going backward in capacity is like taking notes during lecture from back to front - you reference what was already written, not the new notes.
